quizzes.title: W05 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Cain, Abel, Lamech, and the contrast between Cain’s line and Seth’s line

questions.question: What does the lecture emphasize more than the reason God preferred Abel’s offering?
questions.explanation: The lecture focuses less on why Abel’s offering was accepted and more on Cain’s angry response and moral accountability.

questions.question: In Genesis 4, what does the image of sin 'crouching at the door' portray?

options.text: A predatory force waiting to seize Cain

questions.explanation: The summary says sin is personified as an ambusher lying in wait.

questions.question: Which pair of terms in Genesis 4 intentionally echoes Genesis 3 according to the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture highlights the repeated desire/rule language as an intentional echo of Genesis 3.

questions.question: How is Cain’s genealogy structured in the lecture’s description?

options.text: As a segmented genealogy of tribes
As a linear genealogy building toward Lamech

questions.explanation: The genealogy is described as linear, tracing one key figure per generation until Lamech.

questions.question: What makes Lamech the climax of Cain’s line in Genesis 4?

options.text: He boasts of escalating violence and takes two wives
He calls on the name of the LORD

questions.explanation: Lamech is marked by polygamy and a poetic boast that escalates vengeance beyond Cain.

questions.question: What contrast closes Genesis 4 in the lecture’s reading?

options.text: Two lines: one marked by violence and one by worship

questions.explanation: The ending contrasts Cain’s violent line with Seth’s line, in which people begin calling on the name of the LORD.

quizzes.title: W05 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Genesis 5 as a bridge to creation, Enoch as the seventh, and Noah as the tenth

questions.question: Why does Genesis 5:1–2 matter so much in the lecture’s reading?

options.text: It repeats Genesis 1 language about image, male and female, and blessing

questions.explanation: The lecture says Genesis 5 intentionally reconnects the story to Genesis 1 by reusing creation language.

questions.question: What repeated formula dominates most entries in Genesis 5?

options.text: Lived, fathered, had other sons and daughters, and died

questions.explanation: The genealogy follows a tight repetitive pattern that ends with 'and he died.'

questions.question: Which figure is highlighted as the seventh generation and breaks the normal death pattern?
questions.explanation: Enoch is spotlighted as the seventh generation and does not receive the normal 'and he died' ending.

questions.question: What unusual statement replaces 'and he died' in Enoch’s case?

options.text: He was no more, because God took him
He called on the name of the LORD

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights Enoch’s exceptional outcome: God took him.

questions.question: How is Noah specially marked at the tenth generation?

options.text: By a naming explanation tied to comfort from the cursed ground

questions.explanation: Noah’s naming is linked to comfort/rest from toil and pain caused by the curse on the ground.

questions.question: What major structural observation does the lecture make about Genesis 5?

options.text: It ends before the flood begins
The flood narrative is inserted into the genealogy, which closes only at Genesis 9:29
It has no link to Genesis 6

questions.explanation: The summary explicitly says the genealogy remains open through the flood narrative and concludes at Genesis 9:29.

quizzes.title: W05 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Noah wordplay, divine grief, grace, and the contextual reading of Genesis 6:1–4

questions.question: What literary feature does the lecture emphasize around Noah in Genesis 5–6?

options.text: Extended Hebrew wordplay linking Noah, comfort, grief, and grace

questions.explanation: The summary highlights a network of Hebrew wordplays around Noah, nacham, and chen.

questions.question: What does Genesis 6:5 mark in the lecture’s interpretation?

options.text: A reversal from God seeing what is good to seeing pervasive human evil

questions.explanation: The lecture says Genesis 6:5 reverses the earlier creation refrain by showing God seeing great wickedness.

questions.question: Which Hebrew term is tied both to Noah’s naming in Genesis 5:29 and to God’s grief/regret in Genesis 6:6–7?
questions.explanation: The summary connects Noah’s promised comfort with the same root used for God’s grief/comforting in Genesis 6.

questions.question: What sharp contrast follows God’s judgment declaration in Genesis 6:5–7?
questions.explanation: The narrative pivots with 'But Noah,' who finds chen—grace or favor—in God’s eyes.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe Noah in a way that parallels Enoch?
questions.explanation: Noah is described as righteous and as one who walked with God, paralleling Enoch.

questions.question: How does the lecture most likely read the 'sons of God' in Genesis 6:1–4 within the immediate context?

options.text: As a symbolic reference to animals
As part of a human-centered reading tied to lineage mixing and escalating arrogance

questions.explanation: The lecture favors a contextual human-centered reading tied to multiplication, lineages, and human self-assertion.

quizzes.title: W05 Quiz 4
quizzes.description: Exodus 19 as the turning point to law and the method of composition criticism

questions.question: How does the lecture describe Exodus 19 in the flow of the Pentateuch?

options.text: As the final song of Moses
As a pivot from deliverance to the giving of the law

questions.explanation: Exodus 19 is presented as the major transition from exodus deliverance to the legal material.

questions.question: Why is the Sabbath command used as a test case in the lecture?

options.text: To prove Christians never discuss the law
To show the legal code is straightforwardly observed in full today
To raise questions about how biblical law remains binding
To reject the Ten Commandments entirely

questions.explanation: The Sabbath example is used to probe how and why some laws are considered binding today.

questions.question: What method will be used to analyze Exodus 19?
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly introduces composition criticism as the method for reading Exodus 19.

questions.question: According to the lecture, composition criticism studies what?

options.text: Selection, arrangement, adaptation, and writing of texts into a purposeful whole

questions.explanation: The method is defined in those terms in the summary.

questions.question: What assignment are students told to complete before the next lecture?

options.text: Write a paper on Sinai geography
Make at least ten observations on Exodus 19, especially oddities like repetitions, gaps, and inconsistencies

questions.explanation: Students are instructed to produce at least ten observations focused on textual oddities.

questions.question: How are students encouraged to make those observations if they do not know Hebrew?

options.text: By comparing at least three Bible translations

questions.explanation: The lecture tells students to compare multiple translations to notice differences and tensions.
